content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 28, 2008

Ms. Michele Gault
California Commission for Economic Development
Office of Lt. Governor John Garamendi
State Capitol, Room 1114
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-08-127

Dear Ms. Gault:

This letter responds to your request for assistance regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

FACTS

The California Commission for Economic Development ("CED") is an agency located within the Lt. Governor's office. The CED is considering the possibility of organizing a trade and economic development mission to China in February 2009. It is expected that Lt. Governor John Garamendi, as chair of the Commission, would lead the mission. One or more staff members would accompany the Lt. Governor and the trade delegation. We anticipate that up to 100 persons, representing the private sector, non-profit entities, academic institutions and state and local governments would participate in the trade mission. The objective of the trip would be to promote mutual trade, investment and cultural relationships between the State of California and China.

You observe that the CED has the authority to accept gifts and donations to assist it in carrying out its duties and responsibilities (California Government Code Sections 14999-14999.10). The CED special fund currently has a zero balance.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-08-127
Page No. 2

For the purposes of this request for advice, you present the following two
scenarios. In both instances, you ask us to assume that the travel expenses for
representatives from the Lt. Governor's office will be paid for out of the CED's special
fund.

1. The special fund will receive monies from a variety of donors, including
business entities, individuals and non-profit organizations. The donors will contribute to
the CED with the knowledge and understanding that some or all of their respective
donations will be used to defray the costs, including travel and lodging, of the
international trade mission for representatives from the Lt. Governor's office, but will not
be earmarked or identified in advance for allocation to any given individual. You may
also assume for purposes of the analysis, that Company ABC, a manufacturer, contributes
$300, Company XYZ, an agribusiness, contributes $3,000, University LMN, a private
nonprofit university, contributes $2,500, Organization QRS, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit entity,
contributes $15,000, and individual TUV, a private individual who plans to participate in
the mission, contributes $1,500.

2. Assume that 50 individuals plan to participate in the trade mission. They pay a
California-based travel agency for the cost of their trip with the understanding that a
portion (e.g. $500) of the payment will be donated to the CED. The travel agency
collects the proceeds and donates these funds to the CED. The Lt. Governor or the
CED's executive director will then allocate a portion of these funds to cover travel and
lodging costs for representatives of the Lt. Governor who participate in the mission.

You provided the following schedule of the Lt. Governor's (and staff's) activities
during the proposed trip to China:

Wednesday, February 18, 2009
Depart for China.

Thursday, February 19
Arrive in Beijing. Welcome dinner this evening. The Lt. Governor is scheduled to be a
speaker at the dinner event. Dinner will be provided by a Beijing government agency.

Friday, February 20
Full day of meetings with Chinese Government officials. Lunch and dinner would be
provided by a Chinese government agency.

Saturday, February 21
Full day of meetings with Chinese government officials and businesses. Meals provided
by a Chinese government agency.

File No. A-08-127
Page No. 3

**Sunday, February 22**
Sightseeing during the day and dinner meeting with Chinese government officials and
businesses. Meals provided by a Chinese government agency.

**Monday, February 23**
Fly from Beijing to Xi'an. Business and meetings with government officials in Xi'an.

**Tuesday, February 24**
Government meetings in Xi'an. Meals provided by a Xi'an government agency.

**Wednesday, February 25**
Fly from Xi'an to Shanghai. Business and government meetings in Shanghai. Meals
provided by a Shanghai government agency.

**Thursday, February 26**
Business and government meetings all day. Meals provided by a Shanghai government
agency.

**Friday, February 27**
Business and government meetings all day. Meals provided by a Shanghai government
agency.

**Saturday, February 28**
Return flight to California.

Your questions regarding the proposed travel are discussed below.

**ANALYSIS**

The Act prohibits certain public officials from accepting gifts from any single
source in any calendar year with a total value of more than $390.² (Section 89503;
Regulation 18940.2.) The Act also states that any person who *makes a gift in violation of*
the gift limit is liable in a civil action for an amount of up to three times the amount of the
unlawful gift. (Section 89521.) A gift is defined as "any payment that confers a personal
benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not
received." (Section 82028(a).) The Lt. Governor and designated employees in the Lt.
Governor's office are subject to the gift limits of the Act. (Section 89503(a) and (c).)

The Lt. Governor and staff's purpose in making the trip is to promote mutual
trade, investment and cultural relationships between the State of California and China.
The Lt. Governor is scheduled to be a speaker at the mission's welcome dinner in Beijing
on February 19, 2005. The trip includes business and government meetings in Beijing,
Xi'an and Shanghai. Under the Act, any travel is presumed to confer some personal

_______________
² The gift limit is adjusted every two years to reflect changes in the Consumer Price Index.
(Regulation 18940.2(a).)

File No. A-08-127
Page No. 4

benefit on a public official. In addition, travel costs paid for by a third party generally are
reportable gifts under the Act. Therefore, absent an exception, the value of free
transportation to Beijing, Xi'an and Shanghai, meals, accommodations, and car services,
are considered reportable gifts to the Lt. Governor and staff. (Section 82028; Gault
Advice Letter, No. A-07-158.) The questions in your request for advice focus on the
exception to the gift limit for gifts made to government agencies under Regulation
18944.2.

1. Are individual donations to the CED fund, such as those described above,
considered "gifts to an agency," and if so, are they subject to the reporting requirements
described in Regulation 18944.2?

Regulation 18944.2 sets forth circumstances under which a payment made to a
state or local government agency, that is controlled by the agency and used for official
agency business, is not considered a reportable or limited gift to an individual public
official, although the official receives a personal benefit from the payment. The
regulation provides, in part:

"(c) Gift to an Agency. A payment,³ that is otherwise a gift to a public
official, as defined in Section 82028, shall be considered a gift to the
public official's agency and not a gift to the public official if all of the
following requirements are met:

"(1) Agency Controls Use of Payment. The agency head,⁴ or his or her
designee, determines and controls the agency's use of the payment. The
donor may identify a purpose for the payment, but the donor may not
designate by name, title, class, or otherwise, an official who may use the
payment. If the payment will provide a personal benefit to an official, the
agency head, or his or her designee, shall select the individual who will
use it. The agency official who determines and controls the agency's use
of the payment may not select himself or herself as the individual who will
use the payment.

"(2) Official Agency Business. The payment must be used for official
agency business.

"(3) Agency Reports the Gift. Within 30 days after use of the payment,
the agency reports the payment on a form prescribed by the Commission

³ "Payment" means a payment as defined in Section 82044 and includes a monetary payment to
an agency, a loan, gift, or other transfer, and the payment for, or provision of, goods or services to an
agency.

⁴ "Agency head" means an individual in whom the ultimate legal authority of an agency is vested,
or who has been delegated authority to make determinations by the agency for purposes of this regulation.

File No. A-08-127
Page No. 5


Regulation 18944.2 was recently amended to add new restrictions on gifts to
agencies for travel. Subdivision (d) states, in part:

"(d) Limitations on Application of this Regulation. The exception
provided in subdivision (c) does not apply to the following payments:

"(1) A payment for travel, including transportation, lodging, and meals,
for a state or local elected officer, as defined in Section 82020, or an
official specified in Section 87200. 5

"(2) A payment for travel to the extent that it exceeds the agency's
reimbursement rates for travel, meals, and lodging, and other actual and
necessary expenses, or if the agency has no standard policy or practice
concerning reimbursement rates, the State per diem rates as set forth in
applicable sections of the State Administrative Manual and Department of
Personnel Administration regulations, or the Internal Revenue Service
rates for reimbursement of these expenses as set forth in the U.S. General
Service Administration's website under "Per Diem Rates" and Internal
Revenue Service Publications 463 and 1542, or their successors.

"(3) A payment for travel that the agency head, or his or her designee,
has not preapproved in writing in advance of the date of the trip."

Under Regulation 18944.2(d)(1), the exception to the $390 gift limit for certain
gifts to government agencies does not cover third parties' payments for travel by elected
officers and those officials listed in Section 87200. Thus, the donations you have listed
in the two factual scenarios may not be used to cover the cost of travel by the Lt.
Governor for the trade mission to China under the gift to agency regulation.

Donations to the CED fund used for official agency business to defray the costs of
CED staff traveling on a trade mission to China would be considered a gift to an agency
under Regulation 18944.2, if all the other requirements of the regulation are met.
Namely, the agency head or his or her designee must determine and control the use of the
payment, and must be in charge of selecting who will use the payment. The payment
must be used for official agency business. The agency must report the payment on Form
801and post the information on the agency's website. Lastly, payments for travel for

5 Section 87200 includes: "elected state officers, judges and commissioners of courts of the
judicial branch of government, members of the Public Utilities Commission, members of the State Energy
Resources Conservation and Development Commission, members of the Fair Political Practices
Commission, members of the California Coastal Commission, members of planning commissions,
members of the board of supervisors, district attorneys, county counsels, county treasurers, and chief
administrative officers of counties, mayors, city managers, city attorneys, city treasurers, chief
administrative officers and members of city councils of cities, and other public officials who manage public
investments, and to candidates for any of these offices at any election."

File No. A-08-127
Page No. 6

CED staff may only be accepted as a gift to an agency to the extent that they do not
exceed state per diem rates and the payments for travel must be preapproved.

2. Looking at the hypothetical donations to the CED fund listed above, are such
donations allowable by law, and are they subject to any kind of limits?

We may only advise as to whether these donations are permissible or subject to
limits under the Act. Under the Act, donations may be accepted into the CED fund as
gifts to the agency for travel by CED staff if they comply with the requirements of
Regulation 18944.2 described above. Funds donated and used pursuant to the gift to
agency regulation are not subject to limitation as to amount, though they are subject to
the other restrictions of Regulation 18944.2.

In addition, donations of $5,000 or more solicited by the Lt. Governor or his
staff/agents constitute "bequested payments" under Section 82015(b)(2)(B)(iii) of the Act,
copy enclosed. Payments bequested by candidates and elected officials for legislative,
governmental, or charitable purposes are reportable as specified in that section.

3. What reporting obligations, if any, would apply to travel expenses paid on
behalf of the Lt. Governor out of the CED fund, based on the types of donations listed
above?

As discussed above, Regulation 18944.2's exception from the gift limits for gifts
to government agencies was recently amended so that it no longer covers third parties'
payments for travel by an elected officer or a high-level official listed in Section 87200,
such as the Lt. Governor. (Regulation 18944.2(d)(1).) Because donations raised into the
CED fund as a gift to the agency under Regulation 18944.2 may not be used to defray the
costs of travel to China by the Lt. Governor, the obligation to report such funds on a
Form 801 does not arise.

4. What reporting obligations, if any, apply to travel expenses paid on behalf of
CED staff members out of the CED fund, based on the types of donations listed above?

Payments for travel that meet and are accepted pursuant to Regulation 18944.2 as
a gift to an agency must be reported by the agency on Form 801. The information to be
reported is listed in Regulation 18944.2(c)(3).⁶ Under the first scenarios you propose, the

⁶ "(3) Agency Reports the Gift. Within 30 days after use of the payment, the agency reports the
payment or gift presented by the Commission that includes the following information:
"(A) A description of the payment, the date received, the intended purpose, and the amount of the
payment (or the actual or estimated value of the goods or services provided).
"(B) The name and address of the donor. If the donor is not an individual, the report shall also describe
the business activity, or the nature of interests of the entity. If the donor has raised funds from other
persons for the specific purpose of making the payment to the agency, the report shall contain the names of
and amounts given by these persons.

File No. A-08-127
Page No. 7

business entities, individuals and non-profit organizations donating to the CED fund for
the trade and economic development mission to China would be disclosed as "donors" on
the Form 801. (If any of these individuals or entities raised funds from others for the
trade mission, the underlying donors are also disclosed.) Under the second scenario you
described, the 50 individuals participating in the trade mission who donate $500 to the
CED fund would be disclosed individually as donors on the Form 801. A copy of the
form and instructions are enclosed. You may contact the FPPC's Technical Assistance
Division with any questions about completing the Form 801.

If the Lt. Governor's office accepts funds as a gift to the agency, it must provide
the completed Form 801 to the Commission within 30 days after use of the payment, by
mail, personal delivery, electronic mail or facsimile. The Lt. Governor's office must also
post a copy of the form or the information in the form on its website in a prominent
fashion within 30 days after use of the payment. (Regulation 18944.2(c)(3)(E).)

5. How do such reporting obligations, if any, relate to the reporting of income or
gifts on Schedule E of an official's Form 700 Statement of Economic Interests? Should
other forms be used to meet the reporting obligations?

Gifts to an official are reported on the official's Form 700 Statement of Economic
Interests. A payment for travel is reported either on an official's Form 700 Statement of
Economic Interests if it is a gift to the official of $390 or less, or on the agency's Form
801 if it meets the requirements for a gift to an agency under Regulation 18944.2; the
payment is not reported on both forms.¹

6. With respect to meals provided to the Lt. Governor and CED staff by Chinese
government entities, would those expenses be reportable? If so, would they be subject to
the gift limits?

Generally, meals totaling $50 or more from a single source are reportable on an
official's statement of economic interests and are subject to the $390 per year limit from
a single source. However, meals provided to the Lt. Governor and CED staff by Chinese
government entities would fall within the exception of Section 89506(a)(2) for travel,
including subsistence, provided by a foreign government.

"(C) The agency's use of the payment, and the name, title, and department of the agency official for
whom the payment was used. The report shall include the date(s) and place(s) of travel, and a breakdown of
the total expenses for transportation, lodging, meals and other related expenses.
"(D) The form is signed by the agency head, or his or her designee, and maintained by the agency as a
public record subject to inspection and copying under Section 81008(a)."

¹ If the Lt. Governor separately received gifts from donors of less than $390 to fund his travel to
China, he would report these donors on Schedule E of his Form 700 Statement of Economic Interests. It is
important that such gifts be less than $390; any more than $390 is a violation of the gift limits, and gifts of
$390 exactly would create a disqualifying conflict of interest on governmental decisions involving the
donor. (Section 87103(e).)

File No. A-08-127
Page No. 8

Section 89506, governing travel, provides in part:

"(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state,
national, or international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by
this chapter if either of the following apply:

"(1) The travel is in connection with a speech given by the elected state
officer, local elected officeholder, candidate for elected state office or
local elected office, an individual specified in Section 87200, member of a
state board or commission, or designated employee of a state or local
government agency, the lodging and subsistence expenses are limited to
the day immediately preceding, the day of, and the day immediately
following the speech, and the travel is within the United States.

"(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a
foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or
private educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue
and Taxation Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person
domiciled outside the United States which substantially satisfies the
requirements for tax-exempt status under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal
Revenue Code.

Under this section, the meals provided to the Lt. Governor and CED staff are
reportable on their statements of economic interest, but are not subject to limit.

In addition, on February 19, the Lt. Governor is scheduled to be a speaker at the
welcome dinner event hosted by a Beijing government agency. Under Regulation
18950.3, meals and beverages and other nominal benefits an official receives in
connection with an event at which the official gives a speech are not subject to limits and
not reportable.8

7. Use of Campaign Funds for Travel.

When we talked by phone you mentioned that several alternatives for funding the
trip were explored, in addition to accepting donations into the CED funds as a "gift
to agency" under Regulation 18944.2. As we discussed, an elected official may use

8 Regulation 18950.3 provides: "Free admission, and refreshments and similar non-cash nominal
benefits provided to a filer during the entire event at which the filer gives a speech, participates in a panel
or seminar, or provides a similar service and actual intrastate transportation and any necessary lodging and
subsistence provided directly in connection with the speech, panel, seminar, or service, including but not
limited to meals and beverages on the day of the activity, are not payments and need not be reported by any
filer." Regulation 18932.4(g) makes clear that such payments do not constitute prohibited honoraria.

File No. A-08-127
Page No. 9

campaign funds for travel expenditures that are directly related to a political, legislative
or governmental purpose. The use of campaign funds is governed by Sections 89510 to
89518 of the Act. Section 89513(a) addresses the use of campaign funds for travel
expenses, stating:

"(a)(1) Campaign funds shall not be used to pay or reimburse the
candidate, the elected officer, or any individual or individuals with
authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a
committee, or employees or staff of the committee or the elected officer's
governmental agency for travel expenses and necessary accommodations
except when these expenditures are directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose.

"(2) For the purposes of this section, payments or reimbursements for
travel and necessary accommodations shall be considered as directly
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose if the payments
would meet standards similar to the standards of the Internal Revenue
Service pursuant to Sections 162 and 274 of the Internal Revenue Code for
deductions of travel expenses under the federal income tax law."
(Emphasis added.)

Internal Revenue Code Section 162(a)(2) sets forth general rules which permit the
deduction of all ordinary and necessary expenses while traveling away from home in the
pursuit of a trade or business. Under the Act, these rules have been applied to permit the
use of campaign funds for travel to and from the destination where political, legislative or
governmental business will be conducted, and for the portions of a trip when such
business is conducted. To the extent that any day is not a business day (such as days on
which there are no meetings or formal activities), campaign funds may not be used. We
note that the proposed agenda shows governmental business scheduled for all days of the
trade mission to China. When a weekend falls between business days, the weekend will
also be counted as business time. (Bass Advice Letter, No. A-90-474.)

Expenditures for solely personal purposes such as personal recreational activities,
sightseeing, or funding the trip, do not meet the standard of being directly related to
legislative or governmental purposes and may not be paid for with campaign funds.
(Hosterman Advice Letter, No. A-05-044.)

In addition, if campaign funds are used to fund the travel, amounts given by
donors are subject to the contribution limit applicable to the elected official's committee.
Campaign funds used for travel are reported by the elected official's committee on the
relevant campaign statements. The reporting requirements for travel contained in new
Regulation 18421.7 apply. A candidate must briefly describe the political, legislative, or
governmental purpose of expenditures for travel. For an itemized expenditure on travel,
including lodging or a meal, the candidate must also report the dates of travel, the
destination, the goods or services paid for by the expenditure, the number of individuals

File No. A-08-127
Page No. 10

for whom the expenditure was paid, and whether these individuals included the
candidate, a member of the candidate's household, or an individual authorized to approve
the expenditures of the committee's campaign funds. (Regulation 18421.7(a)(3),
enclosed.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

' "Agency head" means an individual in whom the ultimate legal authority of an agency is vested.
or who has been delegated authority to make determinations by the agency for purposes of this regulation.
Regulation 18944.2 was recently amended to add new restrictions on gifts to
agencies for travel. Subdivision (d) states, in part:
"(d) Limitations on Application of this Regulation. The exception
provided in subdivision (c) does not apply to the following paymenrs:
"(l) A payment for travel, including transportation, lodging, and meals,
for a state or local elected officer, as defined in Section g2020, or an
official specified in Section 87ZOO.s
"(2) A payment for travel to the extent that it exceeds the agency,s
reimbursement rates for travel, meals, and lodging, and other actual and
necessary expenses, or if the agency has no standard policy or practice
concerning reimbursement rates, the State per diem rates as set forth in
applicable sections of the State Administrative Manual and Department of
Personnel Administration regulations, or the Internal Revenue Service
rates for reimbursement of these expenses as set forth in the U.S. General
Service Administration's website under "Per Diem Rates" and Internal
Revenue Service Publications 463 and 1542, or their successors.
"(3) A payment for travel that the agency head, or his or her designee,
has not preapproved in writing in advance of the date of the trip."
Under Regulation 18944.2(d)(l), the exception to the $390 gift limit for cerrain
gifts to government agencies does not cover third parties' paymentJ for travel by elected
officers and those officials listed in Section 87200. Thus, the donations you have listed
in the two factual scenarios may not be used to cover the cost of travel by the Lt.
Governor for the trade mission to china under the gift to agency regulation.
Donations to the CED fund used for official agency business to defray the costs of
CED staff traveling on a trade mission to China would be considered a gift to an agency
under Regulation 18944.2, if all the other requirements of the regulation are met.
Namely, the agency head or his designee must determine and control the use of the
payment, and must be in charge of selecting who will use the payment. The payment
must be used for official agency business. The agency must report the paymenion Form
80land post the information on the agency's website. Lastly, paymenti for travel for

judicial branch of government. members of the Public Utilities Commission. members of the State Energy

members of the board of supervisors. district attorneys. county ctlunsels, county treasurers. and chief
administrative otficers of counties. mayors, city managers. city attorneys. city ireasurers. chief
administrative otflcers and members of city councils of cities. and othei pubiic ofTicials who manage public

CED staff may only be accepted as a gift to an agency to the extent that they do not
exceed state per diem rates and the payments for travel must be p..opprou.d.
2. Looking at the hvpotheticul clonutiorts to the CED.fund listed crbove, ure such

We may only advise as to whether these donations are permissible or subject to
limits under the Act. Under the Act, donations may be accepted into the CED fund as
gifts to the agency for travel by CED staff if they comply wiih the requirements of
Regulation 18944.2 described above. Funds donated and used pLlrsuant to the gift to
agency regulation are not subject to limitation as to amount, though they are surbject to
the other restrictions of Regulation 18944.2.
In addition, donations of $5,000 or rnore solicited by the Lt. Governor or his
staff/agents constitute "behested payments" under Section 82015(bx2xBXiii) of the Act,
copy enclosed. Payments behested by candidates and elected officials for legislative,
governmental, or charitable purposes are reportable as specified in that section.
-t. Whctt reporting obligations, if any, would apply to travel expenses paid on
behalf of the Lt. Governor out of the CED firnd, based on the types of donatiins listed
above?
As discussed above, Regulation 18944.2's exception from the gift limits for gifts
to government agencies was recently amended so that it no longer covi.s third parties'
payments for travel by an elected officer or a high-level officiai listed in Section g72OO,
such as the Lt. Governor. (Regulation t5944.2(d)(l).) Because donations raised into the
CED fund as a gift to the agency under Regulation 18944.2 may not be used to defray the
costs of travel to China by the Lt. Governor, the obligation to report such funds on a
Form 801 does not arise.
4. What reporting obligations, tf any, apply to travel expenses paid on behnlf of
CED staff members out of the CED fimd, based on the types of donatiins listed above?
Payments for travel that meet and are accepted pursuant to Regulation 1g944.2 as
a gift to an agency must be reported by the agency on Form 801. Thelnformation to be
reported is listed in Regulation 18944.2(c)(3).6 .Under the first scenarios you propose, rhe

payment on a firrm prescribed by the Commission that includes the following intbrmatitln:
"(A) A description of the payment. the date received, the intended purpose. and the amor"rnt of the
payment (or the actual or estimated value of the goods or services provided).
"(B) The name and address of the donor. If the donor is not an individual. the report shall also describe

and amounts given by these persons.
bttsiness entities, individuals and non-profit organizations donating to the CED fgnd for
the trade and economic development mission to China would be disclosed.as "donors" on
the Form 801. (If any of these individuals or entities raised funds from others for the
trade mission, the underlying donors are also disclosed.) Under the second scenario you

CED fund would be disclosed individually as donors on the Form 801. A copy of the
form and instmctions are enclosed. You may contact the FPPC's Technical Assistance
Division with any questions about completing the Form 801.
If the Lt. Governor's office accepts funds as a gift to the agency, it must provide
the completed Form 801 to the Commission within 30 days after use of the payment, by
mail, personal delivery, electronic mail or facsimile. The Lt. Governor's ofiice must also
post a copy of the form or the information in the form on its website in a prominent
fashion within 30 days after use of the paymenr. (Regulation rggaa.z@)b)(E).)
5. How do such reporting obligations, tf any, relate to the reporting of inc.ome or
gtfts on Schedule E of an fficial's Fonn 700 Statement of Economic Interists? Should
otherforms be used to meet the reporting obligcttions?
Gifts to an official are reported on the official's Form 700 Statement of Economic
Interests. A payment for travel is reported either on an official's Form 700 Statement of
Economic Interests if it is a gift to the official of $390 or less, or onthe agency's Form
801 if it meets the requirements for a gift to an agency under Regulation ISgq-+.2; the
payment is not reported on both forms.T
6. With respect to meals provided to the Lt. Governor and CED staff by Chinese
Sovernment entities, would those expenses be reportable? If so, would they bi subject to
the gift limits?
Generally, meals totaling $50 or more from a single source are reportable on an
official's statement of economic interests and are subject to the $390 per year limit from
a single source. However, meals provided to the Lt. Governor and CEp itaff by Chinese
government entities would fall within the exception of Section 89506(a)(2) for iravel,
including subsistence, provided by a foreign govemment.
"(C) The agency's use of the payment, and the name. title, and department of the agency official for
whom the payment was used. The report shall include the date(s) and place(s) of travil. and a breakdown of
the total expenses fbr transportation, lodging. meals and other related expenses.
"(D) The ftlrm is signed by the agency head, or his or her designee. and maintained by the a_qency as a
public record subject to inspection and copying under Section g l00g(a)."
' If the Lt. Governor separately received gifts from donors of less than $390 to firnd his travel to
China. he would report these donors on Schedule E of his Form 700 Statement of Economic Interests. It is
imptrrtant that such gitis be less tluut $J90: any more than $390 is a violation of the gifi limits. and gitis of
$390 exactly would create a disqualitying contlict of interest on governmental decisions involving the
donor. (Section 87103(e).)
Section 89506, governing travel, provides in part:
"(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state,
national, or international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by
this chapter if either of the following apply:
"(1) The travel is in connection with a speech given by the elected state
officer, local elected officeholder, candidate for elected state office or
local elected office, an individual specified in Section g72oo, member of a
state board or commission, or designated employee of a state or local
government agency, the lodging and subsistence expenses are limited to
lhe day immediately preceding, the day of, and the day immediately
following the speech, and the travel is within the United States.
_"(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a
foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or
private educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue
and raxation code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Intemal Revenue code, or by a person
domiciled outside the United States which substantially satisfies the
requirements for tax-exempt status under Section 501(cX3) of the lnternal
Revenue Code.
Under this section, the meals provided to the Lt. Govemor and CED staff are
reportable on their statements of economic interest, but are not subject to limit.
In addition, on February 19, the Lt. Governor is scheduled to be a speaker at the
welcome dinner event hosted by a Beijing government agency. Under Regulation
18950.3, meals and beverages and other nominal benefits an official receives in
connection with an event at which the official gives a speech are not subject to limits and
not reportable.s
7. Use of Ccunpaign Funds for Travel.
When we talked by phone you mentioned that several alternatives for funding the
trip were being explored, inaddition to accepting donations into the CED funds as a,.gift
to agency" under Regulation 18944.2. As we discussed, an elected official may use

benet'its provided to a filer during the entire event at which the filer gives a speech. participates in a panel
or seminar, or provides a similar ser'r'ice and actual intrastate transportation and any'necesiary lodging and
subsistence provided directly in connection with the speech. panel.'.seminar,,,r r"rri.e. including but r,rt
limited to meals and beverages on the day of the activity, are not payments and need not be reported by any
tiler." Regulation ltt932.'1(g) makes clear that such payments do not constitute prohibited honoraria.
campaign funds for travel expenditures that are tlirectly- related to a political, legislcttirle
or Sovenmtental purpose. The use of campaign funds is governed by Sections 89510 to
89518 of the Act. Section 89513(a) addresses the use of campaign fgnds for travel
expenses, stating:
"(a)(l) Campaign f,nds shall not be used to pay or reimburse the
candidate, the elected officer, or any individual or individuals with
authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a
committee, or employees or staff of the committee or the elected officer's
governmental agency for travel expenses and necessary accommodations
except when these expenditures are tlirectly relatecl to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose.
"(2) For the purposes of this section, payments or reimbursements for
travel and necessary accommodations shall be considered as directly
related to a political, legislative, or govemmental purpose if the payments
would meet standards similar to the standards of the lnternal Revenue
Service pursuant to Sections 162 and274 of the Internal Revenue Code for
deductions of travel expenses under the federal income tax law.',
(Emphasis added.)
Internal Revenue Code Section I62(a)(2) sets forth general rules which permit the
deduction of all ordinary and necessary expenses while traveling away from home in the
pursuit of a trade or business. Under the Act, these rules have bien applied to permit the
use of campaign funds for travel to and from the destination where political, legislative or
governmental business will be conducted, and for the portions of a irip when such
business is conducted. To the extent that any day is not a business day (such as days on
which there are no meetings or formal activities), campaign funds may not be used. We
note that the proposed agenda shows govemmental business scheduled for all days of the
trade mission to China. When a weekend falls between business days, the weekend will
also be counted as business time. (Bcss Advice Letter, No. e-90-4i+.)
Expenditures for solely personal purposes such as personal recreational activities,
sightseeing, or extending the trip, do not meet the standard of being directly related to
legislative or govemmental purposes and may not be paid for with campaign funds.
(Hostennan Advice Letter, No. 4-05-044.)
In addition, if campaign funds are used to fund the travel, amollnts given by
donors are subject to the contribution limit applicable to the elected official;s committee.
Campaign funds used for travel are reported by the elected official's committee on the
relevant campaign statements. The reporting requirements for travel contained in new
Regulation 18421.7, apply. A candidate must briefly describe the political, legislative, or
governmental purpose of expenditures for travel. For an itemized ixpenditure-on travel,
including lodging or a meal, the candidate mLlst also report the dates 
-of 
travel, the
destination, the goods or services paid for by the expenditure, the number of individuals
for whom the expenditure was paid, and whether these individuals included the
candidate, a member of the candidate's household, or an individual authorized to approve
the expenditures of the committee's campaign funds. (Regulation l}42L 7(a)(3),
enclosed.)
lf you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether donations to the California Commission for Economic Development (CED) to fund travel costs for the Lt. Governor and staff on an international trade mission to China constitute gifts to an agency under FPPC regulations, or whether they are subject to individual gift limits.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Donations to the CED cannot be used to pay for the Lt. Governor's travel costs under the gift-to-agency exception because elected officers are explicitly excluded from that regulation. However, donations used for non-elected staff travel may qualify as gifts to the agency if the CED head controls the use, the payment is for official business, and proper reporting is completed.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document is truncated but contains complete Question and Conclusion sections. The analysis discusses Regulation 18944.2 in detail regarding gifts to agencies and travel restrictions. File number A-08-127 corresponds to the document ID provided.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Are individual donations to the CED fund, such as those described above, considered "gifts to an agency," and if so, are they subject to the reporting requirements described in Regulation 18944.2?

CONCLUSION: Under Regulation 18944.2(d)(1), the exception to the $390 gift limit for certain gifts to government agencies does not cover third parties' payments for travel by elected officers and those officials listed in Section 87200. Thus, the donations may not be used to cover the cost of travel by the Lt. Governor for the trade mission to China under the gift to agency regulation. Donations to the CED fund used for official agency business to defray the costs of CED staff traveling would be considered a gift to an agency under Regulation 18944.2, if all other requirements of the regulation are met.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 August 28, 2008 Ms. Michele Gault California Commission for Economic Development Office of Lt. Governor John Garamendi State Capitol, Room 1114 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-08-127 Dear Ms. Gault: This letter responds to your request for assistance regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ FACTS The California Commission for Economic Development ("CED") is an agency located within the Lt. Governor's office. The CED is considering the possibility of organizing a trade and economic development mission to China in February 2009. It is expected that Lt. Governor John Garamendi, as chair of the Commission, would lead the mission. One or more staff members would accompany the Lt. Governor and the trade delegation. We anticipate that up to 100 persons, representing the private sector, non-profit entities, academic institutions and state and local governments would participate in the trade mission. The objective of the trip would be to promote mutual trade, investment and cultural relationships between the State of California and China. You observe that the CED has the authority to accept gifts and donations to assist it in carrying out its duties and responsibilities (California Government Code Sections 14999-14999.10). The CED special fund currently has a zero balance. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-08-127 Page No. 2 For the purposes of this request for advice, you present the following two scenarios. In both instances, you ask us to assume that the travel expenses for representatives from the Lt. Governor's office will be paid for out of the CED's special fund. 1. The special fund will receive monies from a variety of donors, including business entities, individuals and non-profit organizations. The donors will contribute to the CED with the knowledge and understanding that some or all of their respective donations will be used to defray the costs, including travel and lodging, of the international trade mission for representatives from the Lt. Governor's office, but will not be earmarked or identified in advance for allocation to any given individual. You may also assume for purposes of the analysis, that Company ABC, a manufacturer, contributes $300, Company XYZ, an agribusiness, contributes $3,000, University LMN, a private nonprofit university, contributes $2,500, Organization QRS, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit entity, contributes $15,000, and individual TUV, a private individual who plans to participate in the mission, contributes $1,500. 2. Assume that 50 individuals plan to participate in the trade mission. They pay a California-based travel agency for the cost of their trip with the understanding
embedding.summary: The FPPC advises the California Commission for Economic Development on whether donations to fund an international trade mission to China can avoid gift limits by qualifying as gifts to an agency rather than gifts to individual officials. The FPPC concludes that while staff travel may qualify under the gift-to-agency exception with proper controls, the Lt. Governor's travel cannot be funded this way due to explicit regulatory restrictions on elected officer travel.
